result:
The old Ferris wheel stood rusting at the edge of the fairground, a skeletal monument to summers past. The grass had grown tall around its base, and a single hawk circled the silent peak, as though searching for the lost laughter that once rose from the gondolas. I used to think its stillness was a kind of sadness, a once-bright eye now glazed over. But today, the low angle of the November sun caught the frames just right, casting long, intricate shadows that danced like the hands of a stopped clock. It wasn't a failure. It was a fossil. A beautiful, geometric reminder that every cycle, no matter how dizzying, eventually comes to a natural pause.

We are so afraid of the brake, of the final creak that signals the end of the ride. We cling to the momentum, fearing that the stillness will be a void. But the wheel doesn't mourn the children it no longer lifts. It simply stands, becoming a perch for the hawk, a canvas for the light, a landmark for the wandering clouds. To stop is not to die, but to change form. Perhaps the most graceful thing we can learn is how to become a monument to our own turning, to let the rust be a badge of honor and the silence a new kind of music. The wheel awaits no operator; it simply waits for the sunset.
